evals.prompt: I'm publishing an RTMP stream with H.264+AAC. I want browser viewers to watch via WebRTC. Do I need to configure anything special, and will there be any transcoding?
evals.expected_output: Should explain rtmp_to_rtc config, AAC-to-Opus audio transcoding, H.264 video passthrough, and ideally mention performance impact and playback URL.

assertions.name: mentions_rtmp_to_rtc_config
assertions.description: Answer mentions 'rtmp_to_rtc on' as the required configuration

assertions.name: aac_to_opus_transcoding
assertions.description: Answer explains that AAC audio is automatically transcoded to Opus for WebRTC

assertions.name: h264_no_transcoding
assertions.description: Answer clarifies that H.264 video passes through without transcoding (transmux only)

assertions.name: uses_libavcodec_not_external_ffmpeg
assertions.description: Answer specifies that audio transcoding uses FFmpeg's libavcodec as a linked library, not an external FFmpeg process

assertions.name: performance_impact
assertions.description: Answer mentions that audio transcoding reduces WebRTC capacity (dozens of connections vs hundreds for UDP protocols)

assertions.name: disabled_by_default
assertions.description: Answer mentions that rtmp_to_rtc is disabled by default and must be explicitly enabled

assertions.name: whep_playback
assertions.description: Answer mentions WHEP endpoint or playback URL for WebRTC viewing

assertions.name: no_hallucination
assertions.description: Answer does not claim SRS does video transcoding or mention features not in the knowledge base

evals.prompt: I just cloned the SRS repo on my Linux box. How do I build it, push a test stream, and watch it in my browser?
evals.expected_output: Should provide build steps, run command with console.conf, FFmpeg publish command using doc/source.flv, browser playback URLs (HTTP-FLV/HLS), correct ports, and mention HTTP API or SRS console for checking streams.

assertions.name: build_steps
assertions.description: Answer includes build steps: cd srs/trunk, ./configure, and make

assertions.name: run_with_console_conf
assertions.description: Answer shows running SRS with console.conf (./objs/srs -c conf/console.conf)

assertions.name: ffmpeg_publish_with_test_file
assertions.description: Answer shows FFmpeg publish command using the bundled doc/source.flv test file

assertions.name: rtmp_publish_url
assertions.description: Answer includes RTMP publish URL like rtmp://localhost/live/livestream

assertions.name: browser_playback_urls
assertions.description: Answer provides browser playback options: HTTP-FLV (.flv) and/or HLS (.m3u8) URLs

assertions.name: correct_ports
assertions.description: Answer mentions correct default ports: RTMP 1935, HTTP 8080, or API 1985

assertions.name: api_or_console_for_checking
assertions.description: Answer mentions HTTP API or SRS console for checking/monitoring streams

assertions.name: no_hallucination
assertions.description: Answer does not include hallucinated steps like npm install, Docker required, or incorrect commands

evals.prompt: My network is unstable with frequent packet loss. Should I use SRT or RTMP to push a live stream to SRS? What's the difference?
evals.expected_output: Should recommend SRT for unstable networks, explain TCP vs UDP difference, mention srt_to_rtmp conversion, and note tool support.

assertions.name: recommend_srt_for_unstable_network
assertions.description: Answer recommends SRT for unstable networks because it handles packet loss better than RTMP

assertions.name: tcp_vs_udp_transport
assertions.description: Answer explains that RTMP uses TCP and SRT uses UDP

assertions.name: srt_version_and_libsrt
assertions.description: Answer mentions SRT supported since v4.0 and/or uses libsrt

assertions.name: srt_to_rtmp_conversion
assertions.description: Answer mentions srt_to_rtmp config for converting SRT to RTMP for downstream playback (HLS, HTTP-FLV, etc.)

assertions.name: tool_support
assertions.description: Answer mentions that common tools like OBS and FFmpeg support both SRT and RTMP

assertions.name: no_hallucination
assertions.description: Answer does not hallucinate features not in the knowledge base

evals.prompt: Does SRS support H.265? Which protocols can I use with HEVC streams?
evals.expected_output: Should list H.265 support since v6.0, enumerate supported protocols, mention WebRTC HEVC in v7.0, and clarify no video transcoding.

assertions.name: hevc_since_v6
assertions.description: Answer mentions H.265/HEVC supported since v6.0 via Enhanced RTMP

assertions.name: supported_protocols
assertions.description: Answer lists protocols that support HEVC: RTMP, HTTP-FLV, HTTP-TS, HLS, MPEG-DASH, SRT, GB28181, DVR

assertions.name: hls_fmp4_v7
assertions.description: Answer mentions HLS fMP4/LLHLS support for HEVC in v7.0

assertions.name: webrtc_hevc_v7
assertions.description: Answer mentions WebRTC HEVC supported in v7.0 with Safari and/or Chrome 136+ playback

assertions.name: no_video_transcoding
assertions.description: Answer clarifies that SRS transmuxes H.265 video without re-encoding (no built-in video transcoding)

assertions.name: no_hallucination
assertions.description: Answer does not hallucinate unsupported HEVC features or incorrect protocol support

evals.prompt: I need to serve 10,000 RTMP viewers. SRS is single-threaded — how do I scale it?
evals.expected_output: Should explain edge cluster for scaling viewers (deprecated, RTMP only), distinguish from origin cluster (for more streams), mention single-threaded design, and HLS cluster option.

assertions.name: single_threaded_design
assertions.description: Answer explains SRS is single-threaded by design with no multi-threading support

assertions.name: edge_cluster_for_viewers
assertions.description: Answer mentions edge cluster as the solution for scaling viewers

assertions.name: edge_cluster_deprecated_new_planned
assertions.description: Answer notes that edge cluster is currently deprecated (only supports RTMP), but a new version is planned

assertions.name: origin_cluster_distinction
assertions.description: Answer distinguishes origin cluster as being for extending the number of streams, not for scaling viewers

assertions.name: tcp_performance
assertions.description: Answer mentions that TCP protocols (RTMP, HTTP-FLV) support thousands of connections per instance

assertions.name: hls_cluster_option
assertions.description: Answer mentions HLS cluster (built with Nginx) as an alternative for HLS viewers, since v5.0

assertions.name: no_hallucination
assertions.description: Answer does not hallucinate multi-threading config or incorrect clustering features

evals.prompt: I'm choosing between SRS and Nginx-RTMP for a live streaming project. We need RTMP ingest and multiple playback options. Which one should I pick?
evals.expected_output: Should compare protocol coverage, maintenance status, Enhanced RTMP support, and be objective about both.

assertions.name: nginx_rtmp_protocol_limits
assertions.description: Answer mentions Nginx-RTMP supports RTMP and HLS, but not WebRTC, SRT, or HTTP-FLV

assertions.name: srs_protocol_coverage
assertions.description: Answer mentions SRS supports RTMP, HLS, HTTP-FLV, WebRTC, SRT, MPEG-DASH, and more

assertions.name: both_support_rtmp_ingest
assertions.description: Answer notes both support RTMP ingest from OBS/FFmpeg

assertions.name: enhanced_rtmp
assertions.description: Answer mentions SRS supports Enhanced RTMP (e.g., HEVC over RTMP) and keeps up with RTMP protocol evolution

assertions.name: maintenance_status
assertions.description: Answer mentions SRS is actively maintained (community and AI-assisted) while Nginx-RTMP has not been maintained for about a decade

assertions.name: objective_comparison
assertions.description: Answer is objective and acknowledges Nginx-RTMP strengths (e.g., Nginx ecosystem integration)

assertions.name: no_hallucination
assertions.description: Answer does not hallucinate features about either server

evals.prompt: I have IP cameras that push GB28181 streams. Can SRS receive them? What do I need besides SRS?
evals.expected_output: Should explain GB28181 support, external srs-sip requirement, downstream delivery options, and v5 vs v6 differences.

assertions.name: gb28181_publishing_support
assertions.description: Answer confirms SRS supports receiving/publishing GB28181 streams

assertions.name: ps_over_tcp
assertions.description: Answer mentions GB28181 uses PS (Program Stream) over TCP

assertions.name: external_srs_sip_required
assertions.description: Answer mentions SRS requires an external SIP server (srs-sip) for SIP signaling in v6+

assertions.name: downstream_delivery
assertions.description: Answer mentions GB28181 streams can be converted to RTMP for downstream delivery (HLS, HTTP-FLV, WebRTC, etc.)

assertions.name: v5_vs_v6_rewrite
assertions.description: Answer mentions GB28181 supported since v5.0 but completely rewritten in v6 — v5 had an embedded SIP server, v6 removed it and requires external srs-sip

assertions.name: no_hallucination
assertions.description: Answer does not hallucinate features like a built-in SIP server in v6+

evals.prompt: I keep hearing "transmuxing" and "transcoding" — what's the difference? Which one does SRS do?
evals.expected_output: Should explain transmuxing vs transcoding, SRS focus on transmuxing, built-in audio-only transcoding, and external FFmpeg for video transcoding.

assertions.name: transmuxing_definition
assertions.description: Answer explains transmuxing = repackaging media into a different protocol format without re-encoding

assertions.name: transcoding_definition
assertions.description: Answer explains transcoding = re-encoding media to a different codec

assertions.name: srs_focuses_on_transmuxing
assertions.description: Answer states SRS focuses on transmuxing, not transcoding

assertions.name: builtin_audio_transcoding_only
assertions.description: Answer mentions SRS has built-in audio transcoding only (AAC<->Opus) for WebRTC interop

assertions.name: no_builtin_video_transcoding
assertions.description: Answer clarifies no built-in video transcoding — use external FFmpeg for that

assertions.name: external_ffmpeg_transcoding
assertions.description: Answer mentions SRS has an external transcoding feature that uses FFmpeg

assertions.name: no_hallucination
assertions.description: Answer does not hallucinate transcoding features

evals.prompt: I'm on Windows 10. Can I install and run SRS directly?
evals.expected_output: Should explain Linux-only, v5 Windows history, WSL/Docker workarounds, and future possibility.

assertions.name: currently_linux_only
assertions.description: Answer states SRS is currently Linux-only and does not run natively on Windows

assertions.name: v5_windows_history
assertions.description: Answer mentions SRS v5 had Windows support but it was removed in v6 because AI couldn't maintain it

assertions.name: future_windows_possible
assertions.description: Answer mentions Windows support may return in the future if AI becomes powerful enough to maintain it

assertions.name: wsl_workaround
assertions.description: Answer suggests using WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux) to run SRS on Windows

assertions.name: macos_for_dev
assertions.description: Answer mentions macOS works for development but Linux is the target platform

assertions.name: docker_option
assertions.description: Answer mentions Docker as another option for running SRS on non-Linux systems

assertions.name: no_hallucination
assertions.description: Answer does not hallucinate a Windows installer or native Windows build

evals.prompt: I'm streaming a live event and want to save a recording. Can SRS record streams to files?
evals.expected_output: Should explain DVR feature, VOD use, HLS as recording with on_hls callback, and external FFmpeg option.

assertions.name: dvr_support
assertions.description: Answer mentions SRS supports DVR — recording streams to files

assertions.name: vod_use
assertions.description: Answer mentions recorded files can be used as VOD (Video on Demand)

assertions.name: dvr_since_v1
assertions.description: Answer mentions DVR supported since v1.0

assertions.name: hls_as_recording
assertions.description: Answer mentions HLS can be used as a form of recording (segments to .ts files) with on_hls callback to notify your system to manage the files

assertions.name: external_ffmpeg_recording
assertions.description: Answer mentions using FFmpeg to pull streams from SRS and record externally

assertions.name: no_hallucination
assertions.description: Answer does not hallucinate recording features

evals.prompt: I want the lowest possible latency for my viewers. What protocol should I use in SRS? What's the latency difference between HLS and WebRTC?
evals.expected_output: Should compare latency across protocols, warn about player-side latency trap, and mention SRT as low-latency option.

assertions.name: webrtc_lowest_latency
assertions.description: Answer states WebRTC has the lowest latency — sub-second, real-time

assertions.name: srt_low_latency
assertions.description: Answer mentions SRT also offers low latency — around 500ms to 1s, widely used in live streaming

assertions.name: http_flv_latency
assertions.description: Answer mentions HTTP-FLV has around 1 second latency

assertions.name: hls_latency
assertions.description: Answer mentions HLS has 3-5 seconds latency due to segmenting

assertions.name: player_side_latency_trap
assertions.description: Answer warns that latency is an end-to-end problem and the player matters — VLC adds huge latency even with low-latency protocols, use browsers with WebRTC for true low latency

assertions.name: no_hallucination
assertions.description: Answer does not hallucinate latency numbers or claim sub-second HLS

evals.prompt: I want to live stream from my iPhone to SRS. What app should I use and what protocol?
evals.expected_output: Should recommend Larix Broadcaster, mention browser WHIP option, and suggest protocols.

assertions.name: larix_broadcaster
assertions.description: Answer recommends Larix Broadcaster as a mobile streaming app for iOS/Android supporting RTMP, SRT, WHIP

assertions.name: browser_whip_option
assertions.description: Answer mentions publishing via browser using WHIP (WebRTC) as an option

assertions.name: rtmp_simplest
assertions.description: Answer mentions RTMP as the simplest protocol option for mobile publishing

assertions.name: srt_for_unstable
assertions.description: Answer mentions SRT as a good option if the network is unstable

assertions.name: no_hallucination
assertions.description: Answer does not hallucinate an SRS mobile app or claim OBS works on phones

evals.prompt: I don't want everyone to watch my stream. How do I control who can publish or play in SRS?
evals.expected_output: Should explain HTTP callback auth, IP allow/deny lists, HTTP API for kicking streams, and no built-in user management.

assertions.name: http_callback_auth
assertions.description: Answer mentions HTTP callback for authentication — listen to publish/play events and accept or reject clients

assertions.name: ip_allow_deny_list
assertions.description: Answer mentions IP allow list and deny list for basic security

assertions.name: http_callback_since_v2
assertions.description: Answer mentions HTTP callback supported since v2.0

assertions.name: http_api_kickoff
assertions.description: Answer mentions HTTP API can be used to kick off/stop streams or disconnect publishers/viewers

assertions.name: no_builtin_user_management
assertions.description: Answer clarifies no built-in username/password system — use HTTP callback to integrate with your own auth service

assertions.name: no_hallucination
assertions.description: Answer does not hallucinate built-in user management features

evals.prompt: I want to try SRS quickly without building from source. Can I run it in Docker?
evals.expected_output: Should explain Docker support, docker.conf, environment variables, ports to expose including UDP, and same publish/play workflow.

assertions.name: docker_supported
assertions.description: Answer confirms SRS supports Docker deployment

assertions.name: docker_conf
assertions.description: Answer mentions using conf/docker.conf for Docker configuration (e.g., no daemon mode)

assertions.name: env_variables
assertions.description: Answer mentions SRS supports environment variables for configuration — useful for Docker/cloud-native deployments

assertions.name: ports_to_expose
assertions.description: Answer mentions ports to expose: RTMP 1935, HTTP 8080, API 1985, and UDP ports for SRT and WebRTC

assertions.name: same_publish_play
assertions.description: Answer mentions you can publish and play the same way as a native build

assertions.name: no_hallucination
assertions.description: Answer does not hallucinate incorrect Docker image names or commands

evals.prompt: I see SRS supports WebRTC. Can I use it to build a video conferencing app like Zoom?
evals.expected_output: Should explain SRS can work for small-scale conferencing but the main challenge is the application layer, not the SFU.

assertions.name: small_scale_possible
assertions.description: Answer explains SRS can work for very small-scale conferencing (1-to-1, 2-3 participants) via browser or smartphone

assertions.name: sfu_stream_count
assertions.description: Answer explains SRS is an SFU — for 3 participants that means 9 streams to forward

assertions.name: application_layer_is_main_challenge
assertions.description: Answer explains the biggest challenge for conferencing is not the SFU but the application layer: chat, hand-raising, recording (needs transcoding), SIP integration, etc.

assertions.name: no_turn_no_p2p
assertions.description: Answer mentions SRS does not support TURN or P2P

assertions.name: not_for_large_scale
assertions.description: Answer clarifies SRS is not the right fit for large-scale conferencing

assertions.name: no_hallucination
assertions.description: Answer does not hallucinate built-in room management, MCU, or conferencing features

evals.prompt: WebRTC works fine on localhost, but when I try to publish from my phone on another network, it fails to connect. What's wrong?
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose candidate misconfiguration, HTTPS requirement, UDP firewall, and provide verification steps.

assertions.name: candidate_misconfiguration
assertions.description: Answer identifies candidate misconfiguration as the most likely cause — rtc_server candidate must be set to the server's public IP, not 127.0.0.1 or a private IP

assertions.name: https_required
assertions.description: Answer explains HTTPS is required for WebRTC from non-localhost browsers — without HTTPS, the browser blocks getUserMedia/camera access

assertions.name: udp_firewall
assertions.description: Answer mentions checking that UDP port 8000 is open in the firewall, since WebRTC uses UDP by default

assertions.name: verification_steps
assertions.description: Answer provides concrete verification steps such as curl to check API connectivity, or checking the candidate value in the SDP response

assertions.name: references_webrtc_doc
assertions.description: Answer references or draws information from the WebRTC documentation (webrtc.md or the Connection Failures section)

assertions.name: no_hallucination
assertions.description: Answer does not hallucinate TURN server setup or P2P configuration (SRS does not support either)

evals.prompt: My HLS stream has about 30 seconds of latency. I need it under 10 seconds. How do I reduce it?
evals.expected_output: Should explain HLS segment/window tuning, GOP alignment, player buffering, and suggest alternative protocols for even lower latency.

assertions.name: hls_fragment_config
assertions.description: Answer mentions reducing hls_fragment (segment duration) to a smaller value like 2 seconds

assertions.name: hls_window_config
assertions.description: Answer mentions reducing hls_window to limit the number of segments in the playlist

assertions.name: gop_keyframe_alignment
assertions.description: Answer explains the encoder's GOP/keyframe interval should match or be smaller than the fragment duration

assertions.name: player_side_buffering
assertions.description: Answer mentions player-side buffering as a factor — some players buffer aggressively regardless of server settings

assertions.name: alternative_protocols
assertions.description: Answer suggests alternative protocols for lower latency: HTTP-FLV (~1s) or WebRTC (sub-second) if HLS latency is still too high

assertions.name: realistic_hls_limits
assertions.description: Answer sets realistic expectations — HLS can be tuned to about 5-10 seconds but is not a true low-latency protocol

assertions.name: no_hallucination
assertions.description: Answer does not claim sub-second HLS or hallucinate LL-HLS features not in SRS

evals.prompt: How do I check if SRS is receiving my stream? I published with FFmpeg but nothing seems to be playing.
evals.expected_output: Should guide user through HTTP API diagnostics, log checking, and common causes of stream-not-found.

assertions.name: http_api_streams_check
assertions.description: Answer shows checking active streams via HTTP API: curl http://localhost:1985/api/v1/streams

assertions.name: check_srs_running
assertions.description: Answer suggests verifying SRS is running and listening on the expected ports

assertions.name: url_mismatch
assertions.description: Answer mentions checking that the publish URL and play URL match exactly (app name and stream name)

assertions.name: publish_before_play
assertions.description: Answer mentions that the stream must be published before it can be played

assertions.name: check_logs
assertions.description: Answer suggests checking SRS logs for errors or connection information

assertions.name: no_hallucination
assertions.description: Answer does not hallucinate diagnostic tools or API endpoints that don't exist

evals.prompt: I deployed SRS behind Nginx as a reverse proxy. RTMP works fine but HTTP-FLV streaming is broken — the player connects but no video appears. What's wrong?
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose chunked transfer encoding issues with Nginx reverse proxy and provide correct proxy config.

assertions.name: chunked_transfer_issue
assertions.description: Answer identifies that HTTP-FLV requires chunked transfer encoding and Nginx proxy settings may break it (e.g., proxy_buffering must be off)

assertions.name: nginx_proxy_config
assertions.description: Answer provides or references Nginx proxy configuration for HTTP-FLV (proxy_pass, proxy_buffering off, or similar)

assertions.name: rtmp_unaffected
assertions.description: Answer explains why RTMP still works — RTMP uses its own TCP connection on port 1935, not going through the HTTP reverse proxy

assertions.name: references_http_server_doc
assertions.description: Answer references or draws information from http-server.md or nginx-for-hls.md documentation

assertions.name: no_hallucination
assertions.description: Answer does not hallucinate Nginx modules or SRS config options that don't exist

evals.prompt: I'm using VLC to play an RTMP stream from SRS and the latency is about 30 seconds. Is SRS slow?
evals.expected_output: Should explain the VLC latency trap — VLC adds significant client-side buffering, SRS itself has low RTMP latency.

assertions.name: vlc_buffering_is_the_cause
assertions.description: Answer explains that VLC adds significant client-side buffering and this is the primary cause of the observed latency, not SRS

assertions.name: vlc_not_reliable_for_latency
assertions.description: Answer states VLC is not a reliable reference for evaluating low-latency playback

assertions.name: srs_rtmp_actual_latency
assertions.description: Answer mentions SRS RTMP actual latency is much lower — typically 1-3 seconds

assertions.name: suggest_alternative_player
assertions.description: Answer suggests using a different player to verify: ffplay, browser with HTTP-FLV, or browser with WebRTC

assertions.name: no_hallucination
assertions.description: Answer does not blame SRS for the latency or hallucinate VLC tuning options that solve the problem

evals.prompt: What ports do I need to open in my cloud server firewall for SRS to work with all protocols?
evals.expected_output: Should list the default ports with TCP/UDP distinction and protocol mapping, including optional WebRTC-over-TCP when enabled.

assertions.name: rtmp_port
assertions.description: Answer mentions RTMP on port 1935 (TCP)

assertions.name: http_api_port
assertions.description: Answer mentions HTTP API on port 1985 (TCP)

assertions.name: http_stream_port
assertions.description: Answer mentions HTTP streaming (HLS, HTTP-FLV) on port 8080 (TCP)

assertions.name: webrtc_udp_port
assertions.description: Answer mentions WebRTC media on port 8000 (UDP) by default

assertions.name: webrtc_tcp_port_optional
assertions.description: Answer mentions optional WebRTC-over-TCP, typically on port 8000 (TCP) when rtc_server.tcp is enabled

assertions.name: srt_udp_port
assertions.description: Answer mentions SRT on port 10080 (UDP)

assertions.name: tcp_vs_udp_distinction
assertions.description: Answer clearly distinguishes which ports need TCP vs UDP — this is critical for firewall configuration

assertions.name: no_hallucination
assertions.description: Answer does not hallucinate incorrect port numbers or protocols
